elements.sent-2411-001: The theory of parton energy loss in quark-gluon plasma (QGP) and its associated jet quenching phenomenology in heavy ion collisions has evolved significantly over the past decade, driven by precise measurements from RHIC and LHC.
elements.sent-2411-002: Despite these advancements, fundamental questions remain, such as how parton energy loss in QGP depends on the medium path-length $L$.
elements.sent-2411-003: In these proceedings, we discuss how this question can be addressed through a data-driven strategy.
elements.sent-2411-004: More specifically, we will exploit the scaling properties of large-$\pt$ hadron spectra in heavy ion collisions, predicted by a simple analytic energy loss model and observed in experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-005: In addition, we show that jet measurements obey one such scaling law, offering the possibility to study jet energy loss in QGP within the same strategy.
elements.sent-2411-006: As discussed in Refs.~\cite{Arleo:2017ntr,Arleo:2022shs , the quenching of hadron spectra, $\RAA(\pt)$, exhibits at large $\pt$ a universal shape as a function of $\pt$: \be \RAA^{h (\pt, \meanepsbar, n) \simeq f(u\equiv \pt/\, n\meanepsbar).}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Fits to the quenching of hadron spectra, measured in heavy ion collisions at RHIC (AuAu collisions) and LHC (XeXe, PbPb), allow for a systematic extraction of the scales $\meanepsbar$ across a broad range of collision energies $\sqrts$ and centrality classes ${\cal C}$, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:allscales (see Ref.~\cite{Arleo:2022shs for more details and for the bibliographical references therein to the data sets).}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{EnergyLossValues_all.pdf} \caption{Energy loss scales $\meanepsbar$ extracted from light ($\pi^0$, $h^\pm$) and heavy ($\jpsi$, $D$ in PbPb at 5.02\TeV ) hadron data in various collision systems and centralities. } \label{fig:allscales}
elements.sent-2411-008: In order to go a step further, it is instructive to relate the $\meanepsbar$ to the relevant physical quantities in heavy ion collisions.
elements.sent-2411-009: In the BDMPS formalism, $\meanepsbar$ should scale according to~\cite{Arleo:2022shs \be \meanepsbar \propto \langle z \rangle\, C_k\, \qzero\, \tau_0^\alpha\,L^{2-\alpha}\,, \ee where $\langle z \rangle$ is the momentum fraction carried by the hadron, $C_k$ is the color charge of the parton $k$, $L$ is the medium path-length and $\qzero$ stands for the transport coefficient at formation time $\tau_0$ ($\tau_0 \ll L$).}
elements.sent-2411-010: Since the values of $\langle z \rangle$ and $C_k$ may differ for heavy hadrons, the analysis will focus on light hadrons from now on.
elements.sent-2411-011: The parameter $\alpha$ entering \eqref{eq:mean_eloss_expanding characterizes the dynamical expansion of the medium, according to $\qhat(\tau) = \qhat_0 \left(\tau_0/\tau\right)^{\alpha}$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The initial transport coefficient $\qzero = \qhat(\tau_0)$ is proportional to the initial parton density $n_0$, which in the Bjorken picture can be estimated as \be n_0 = \frac{1 {\AT \tau_0}\,\left.\dNg\right|_{y=0 \,\simeq\,\frac{3 {2}\,\frac{1 {\AT \tau_0}\,\left.\dNch\right|_{y=0 \, , \ee where $\AT$ is the transverse overlap area of the two crossing nuclei.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Plugging \eqref{eq:densitydNch in \eqref{eq:mean_eloss_expanding leads to the following parametric dependence,~\cite{Arleo:2022shs \be \meanepsbar\, = K \times \, \left(\frac{1 {\AT}\,\dNch \,L^{\beta}\right) \,, \ee with $\beta=2-\alpha$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The geometric quantities $A_\perp$ and $L$ entering Eq.~\eqref{eq:eloss_scaling are determined through an optical Glauber model, assuming hard sphere nuclear densities.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The charged particle multiplicity data $\dd N_\textnormal{ch /\dd \eta$ at mid-rapidity is taken from PHENIX at RHIC and from \mbox{ALICE and CMS at LHC (see~\cite{Arleo:2022shs and references therein).}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The energy loss scales $\meanepsbar$ extracted from the quenching of light hadrons are fitted using Eq.~\eqref{eq:eloss_scaling , with $\beta$ (and $K$) taken as a free parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-017: As can be seen in Figure~\ref{fig:scaling2 , the scaling property expected from \eq{eq:eloss_scaling holds very well for light hadron production in all collision systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The fits leads to $\beta=1.02{\raisebox{0.5ex { $\substack{+0.09 -0.06}$} $ ($\chi^2/\textnormal{ndf =0.51$), where the uncertainties originate from the fit and from the use of alternative Glauber models~\cite{Arleo:2022shs .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The value of $\beta$ proves compatible with unity, that is the BDMPS expectation in QGP experiencing a purely longitudinal expansion (i.e. $\alpha=1$).
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{minipage}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{scaling2.pdf} \caption{Energy loss scales $\meaneps$ in various collision systems as a function of $\dd N_\textnormal{ch}/\dd y\times L^{\beta}/\AT$.} \label{fig:scaling2} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.42\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{v2vspt.pdf} \caption{$v_2/\ecc$ of light hadrons vs. $\pt/n\meanepsbar$. The band corresponds to $\beta=1.02^{+0.09}_{-0.06}$.} \label{fig:v2scaling} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-020: Once the dependence of $\meanepsbar$ with $L$ is empirically determined, it becomes possible to investigate the azimuthal dependence of hadron suppression, from which the $v_2$ coefficient can be computed.
elements.sent-2411-021: Using the universal shape of $\RAA$, its $\phi$ dependence can be modeled as \be \RAA(u, n, \phi) = f\left(u\times \left(L/L(\phi)\right)^\beta, n\right)\,, \ee where $L(\phi)$ is the path-length as a function of the azimuthal angle $\phi$ (with $\phi=0$ being the direction of the impact parameter), approximated as $L(\phi) = L\times\left(1-\ecc\, \cos\left(2\phi\right)\right)$, where the eccentricity is defined as $\ecc = \left( L(\pi/2)-L(0)\right) / \left( L(\pi/2)+L(0) \right)$.
elements.sent-2411-022: To first order in $\ecc$ and using Eq.~\eqref{eq:raa_phi , the elliptic flow coefficient can be approximated as~\cite{Arleo:2022shs \bea \frac{v_2(u, n) {\ecc \simeq \frac\beta{2}\,\frac{\partial \ln f(u, n) {\partial \ln u}\,, \frac{v_2(\pt) {\ecc \simeq \frac{\beta {2}\,\frac{\pt {\RAA(\pt)}\,\frac{\partial \RAA(\pt) {\partial \pt}\,.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In order to check this prediction, $\RAA(\pt, \phi)$ has been computed in each collision system, using our full model including Glauber simulations; this is then fitted to extract $v_2$ values.
elements.sent-2411-024: The model predictions using the $\beta$ value fitted in Fig.~\ref{fig:scaling2 (grey band) and the CMS measurements of $v_2$ in \PbPb collisions~\cite{CMS:2012tqw,CMS:2017xgk are plotted together in Fig.~\ref{fig:v2scaling as a function of the scaling variable $\pt/n\meanepsbar$, where the values of $\meanepsbar$ are determined from the fits of $\RAA$ (see Fig.~\ref{fig:allscales ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The predicted scaling for the different collision systems is clearly apparent in the data, except at the lowest values of $\pt/n\meanepsbar$.
elements.sent-2411-026: The simultaneous calculation of $\RAA$ and $v_2$ in the model and its agreement with measurements of both observables is thus compelling~--~moreover with a path-length dependence compatible with perturbative QCD.
elements.sent-2411-027: In addition, Eq.~\eqref{eq:v2finalb indicates that $v_2$ and $\RAA$ at a given $\pt$ are trivially related for measurements from the same collision system.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In particular, this relation does not involve the knowledge of the energy loss scale $\meanepsbar$ nor that of the function $f$ appearing in~\eqref{eq:v2finala .}
elements.sent-2411-029: It is also worth noting that the normalization uncertainties of $\RAA$ vanish when computing~\eqref{eq:v2finalb .}
elements.sent-2411-030: We would like to check whether it also holds when comparing solely $\RAA$ and $v_2$ measurements, independently of the present energy loss model.
elements.sent-2411-031: The $v_2/\ecc$ measurements are plotted as a function of the slope $\dd\ln\RAA/\dd\ln\pt$ in Fig.~\ref{fig:v2vsRAA (left).}
elements.sent-2411-032: The slope is evaluated with an agnostic fit of CMS $\RAA$ data~\cite{CMS:2012aa,CMS:2016xef ; when the centrality classes for $\RAA$ and $v_2$ did not match, values for combined classes have been interpolated with weights scaling with $N_{coll}$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Although the present precision of the data does not allow yet for a rigorous test of Eq.~\eqref{eq:v2finalb , the correlation between the two measured quantities is clearly apparent (correlation coefficient $\rho=0.78$).}
elements.sent-2411-034: In addition, a fit with the expected function $y=\beta\,x/2$ reproduces fairly the hadron observations, resulting in a value $\beta = 0.94\pm0.04$ (fit uncertainties only) consistent with the value $\beta=1.02{\raisebox{0.5ex { $\substack{+0.09 -0.06}$} $ extracted from the scaling with hadron multiplicity, Eq.~\eqref{eq:eloss_scaling .}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: This gives confidence that the relation between $\RAA$ and $v_2$ at large $\pt$ indeed provides a direct experimental access to the path-length dependence of parton energy loss in QGP.
elements.sent-2411-036: A possible explanation for the slight overshoot in the $50$--$60\%$ centrality class may be back-to-back jet correlations contamination in data~\cite{CMS:2016xef .}
elements.sent-2411-037: In other centrality classes, disagreement may be due to the overestimation of \ecc in the Glauber model presently used, which relies on hard sphere nuclear densities.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{v2-over-Eccentricity_VsRAAlogDerivative_CMS_h_PbPb_ptMinCut.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{v2-over-Eccentricity_VsRAAlogDerivative_ATLAS_j_PbPb_ptMinCut.pdf} \caption{Relation between $v_2/\ecc$ and $\dd\ln\RAA/\dd\ln\pt$ for light hadrons in CMS~\cite{CMS:2012tqw,CMS:2017xgk,CMS:2012aa,CMS:2016xef} (left) and inclusive jets in ATLAS~\cite{ATLAS:2018gwx,ATLAS:2021ktw} (right). The bands $y=\beta\,x/2$ show the expectation from~\eqref{eq:v2finalb}, with the value \mbox{$\beta=1.02^{+0.09}_{-0.06}$} determined from the density scaling fit of hadrons of Eq.~\eqref{eq:eloss_scaling} (grey band, left), and the $\beta$ values extracted from linear fits of the displayed hadron or jet data (blue bands). } \label{fig:v2vsRAA}
elements.sent-2411-038: Although related in principle, hadron and jet measurements in heavy ion collisions are likely to probe different aspects of medium-induced gluon radiation.
elements.sent-2411-039: The former appears as a good proxy of genuine parton energy loss in QGP, the latter probes instead gluon emission off a final state made of multiple particles acting coherently.
elements.sent-2411-040: Consequently, there is no reason a priori that parton and jet energy loss exhibit the same energy and path-length parametric dependence, nor the $\pt$ dependence of $\RAA$ of both processes to be identical.\footnote{The $\RAA$ and $v_2$ of jets have also been discussed at this conference, see Ref.~\cite{Mehtar-Tani:2024jtd,Ogrodnik:2024qug for details.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: It is therefore worthwhile to investigate whether the relation~\eqref{eq:v2finalb between $\RAA$ and $v_2$, derived on rather general grounds, also applies to jet production --~despite the different $\pt$-dependence of $\RAA$~-- and, if so, to determine which value of $\beta$ best matches the data.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The results, using $\RAA$~\cite{ATLAS:2018gwx and $v_2$~\cite{ATLAS:2021ktw ATLAS data in PbPb collisions at $\sqrts=5.02$~TeV, are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:v2vsRAA (right).}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: As for hadrons, the expected function $y=\beta\,x/2$ fits well the data\footnote{The centrality class $0$--$5\%$ for hadrons and $0$--$10\%$ for jets have not been included as the small eccentricity results in large uncertainties on $v_2/\ecc$; in addition, the eccentricity in central collisions depends on density fluctuations that cannot be accounted for in the optical Glauber model we use to compute it.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This is a remarkable indication that parton and jet energy loss share the same parametric, close-to-linear, dependence on the medium path-length.
elements.sent-2411-045: It is also interesting to note that a different path-length dependence of jet energy loss, $\Delta E \propto L^{0.59}$, is obtained in the data-driven approach of Ref.~\cite{Wu:2023azi , while other studies point instead towards a stronger dependence on $L$, $\Delta E \propto L^{1.4}$~\cite{Djordjevic:2018ita and $\Delta E \propto L^{2}$~\cite{Ogrodnik:2024qug .}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: In summary, relating the values of $\meanepsbar$ extracted from $\RAA$ to the hadron multiplicity enables the determination of the $L$ dependence of parton energy loss, $\meanepsbar \propto L^{\beta}$ with $\beta=1.02{\raisebox{0.5ex { $\substack{+0.09 -0.06}$} $, in agreement with a longitudinally expanding QGP.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The $v_2/\ecc$ anisotropy coefficient exhibits the same scaling property as $\RAA$, in both the model and data.
elements.sent-2411-048: Finally we highlight a relation between $v_2/\ecc$ and $\RAA$, predicted in the model and observed in data, which allows for a data-driven extraction of the path-length dependence of parton energy loss.
elements.sent-2411-049: We find that jet data obey the same relation, pointing to a similar path-length dependence of parton and jet energy loss in quark-gluon plasma.
elements.sent-2411-050: The LHC Runs~3 \& 4 should allow for testing with unprecedented precision these scaling properties.
